segments.text: They're doing brain surgery on monarch
butterflies to solve a big mystery. So,
every year, monarch butterflies migrate
thousands of kilometers from the US and
Canada to Mexico. How do they know where
to go? We already know that they use the
sun's position to determine direction.
But what if it's rainy or cloudy? It's
possible that they sense the Earth's
magnetic field. To find out, scientists
are inserting tiny electrodes into their
brains. The devices are smaller than a
human hair and they attach to four
neurons that we think control this
magnetic sense. Don't worry, it doesn't
harm the butterflies. Then they
carefully place the butterflies into a
flight simulator where the researchers
can control the magnetic fields inside
as they fly. And the electrodes are
connected to a computer which allows
them to track their brain activity.
Spikes in brain activity like this might
suggest that the butterfly is sensing
the Earth's magnetic field. But we need
more research to be sure and to figure
out if we have a subconscious magnetic
sense.
